div.p: Alexander by the grace of God king of the Scots sends greeting to all worthy men, clerk and lay, of the whole of his land. Let present and future know us to have given, granted, and by this our charter made firm, for the honour of God and his glorious virgin Mother, as well as for our own salvation and our ancestors’ and successors’, to the monks of Melrose serving God in that place and who will serve there in perpetuity, the fishery on the Tweed which is called the fishery of Selkirk; and seven acres of land with buildings and a meadow contained within those seven acres of land, just as the same seven acres of land of the same monks were enclosed by a ditch and fence on the day of this conveyance; and also a pasture for eight oxen and eight cows in ‘Wauhop’. We have also allowed to the said monks that they, by the view of our foresters at the time, may have and take timber in our forest for the upkeep of their stank in the same fishery. For them to hold and to have for ever in free, pure, and everlasting alms, as freely, quitely, fully, and honourably as any alms out of our gift is freely, quitely, fully and honourably held or possessed by any religious men in our kingdom. As witnesses, the venerable father William, bishop of Glasgow, our chancellor; Philip, abbot of Jedburgh; David of Lindsay, justiciar of Lothian; Bernard of Ripley. At Jedburgh on the eighth day of February, in the thirty-third year of the reign of the lord king [1247].
